item.title: Returning data in PostgreSQL
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
In orôÙder to get the best perôÙforôÙmance from our dataôÙbase enôÙgine, most of the ôÙtimes, we have to take adôÙvanôÙtage of its exôÙcluôÙsive feaôÙtures. This has the disôÙadôÙvanôÙtage of getôÙting farôÙther away from stanôÙdard, ANôÙSI code, and in the case of RDBM enôÙgiôÙnes, this alôÙso meanôÙs, most of the ORMs will not work.

BeôÙsides that, it could someôÙtimes be worth exôÙplorôÙing what we can gain by adaptôÙing to the enôÙgine.

This is someôÙthing learnt from the feedôÙback of my talk givôÙen at EuôÙroPyôÙthon 2016. AfôÙter the talk, an atôÙtendee told me that he liked it, and asked me about the code porôÙtrayed in one of the slides, the one about decôÙoôÙraôÙtors with the exôÙamôÙple of movôÙing some records from one table ôÙto anôÙothôÙer. He asked me if I ran code like that one in proôÙducôÙtion, to which I anôÙswered no: the code is enôÙtireôÙly ficôÙtionôÙal, and it was done for the mere purôÙposôÙes of the exôÙamôÙples, so I just needôÙed an exôÙcuse for a task that could be ôÙdone in two SQL stateôÙments, so the exôÙamôÙple with the decôÙoôÙraôÙtor can be shown.

The code of the exôÙamôÙple folôÙlows:

INSERT INTO archive_orders
SELECT * from orders
WHERE order_date < '2016-01-01';

DELETE from orders WHERE order_date < '2016-01-01';

Then I learnt that there is anôÙothôÙer way of writôÙing that sort of stateôÙments, with the "reôÙturnôÙing" senôÙtence of PostôÙgreSQL.

It would be re-writôÙten like:

WITH deleted as (
    DELETE FROM orders WHERE order_date < '2016-01-01'
    RETURNING *
)
INSERT INTO archive_orders select * from deleted;

Or... the othôÙer way around:

WITH to_delete as(
    INSERT INTO archive_orders
    SELECT id, description, order_date FROM orders WHERE order_date < '2016-01-01'
    RETURNING id
)
DELETE FROM orders where id in (select id from to_delete);

The inôÙterôÙestôÙing point here, is that it enôÙtails a sinôÙgle comôÙmand, inôÙstead of tôÙwo. So this can be done with a sinôÙgle call to the database, savôÙing an exôÙtra round trip.

The point here is that the delete stateôÙment of PostôÙgreSQL (as well as the rest of the stateôÙments, INôÙSERT, UPôÙDATE, for inôÙstance), can be specôÙiôÙfied to reôÙturn the daôÙta they afôÙfecôÙt, and this can be used in an inôÙterôÙmeôÙdiôÙate taôÙble to pipe it to anôÙothôÙer stateôÙmenôÙt.

By deôÙfault, if you run the delete stateôÙmenôÙt, it should reôÙturn the numôÙber of afôÙfectôÙed rows, like:

delete from orders where id > 3;

DELETE 4

But, we can make it to reôÙturn the rows themôÙselves:

delete from orders where id > 3 returning \*;

id|   description    |     order_date
----+------------------+---------------------
4 | to be archived   | 2014-12-09 00:00:00
5 | First sale order | 2016-07-17 00:00:00
6 | First sale order | 2016-07-20 00:00:00
7 | First sale order | 2016-07-24 00:00:00
(4 rows)

DELETE 4

Or, speôÙcifôÙic colôÙumns if we seôÙlect them:

delete from orders where id > 3 returning id, description;

id |   description
----+------------------
4 | to be archived
5 | First sale order
6 | First sale order
7 | First sale order
(4 rows)

DELETE 4

So, we can use the "reôÙturnôÙing" feaôÙture of PostôÙgreSQL, to do in a sinôÙgle comôÙmand what we usuôÙalôÙly would do in two or more, and in some casôÙes, it might be ôÙsomeôÙthing worth exôÙplorôÙing. It was great learnôÙing things like this one, and getôÙting tips as a reôÙsult from the feedôÙback of the talk (it does not change the meanôÙing, and the exôÙamôÙple could reôÙmain the same for the aforeôÙmenôÙtioned reaôÙsonôÙs; it is just an exôÙamôÙple :-).
